primary_diagnosis.name: hEDS musculoskeletal flare — recurrent subluxations (spontaneously reduced) with reactive muscle spasm

pathophysiology_summary: hEDS causes ligamentous laxity and impaired proprioception, producing recurrent joint subluxations/dislocations with reactive protective muscle spasm and chronic myofascial pain, often with early osteoarthritis. In this flare the left shoulder and right patella subluxed and spontaneously reduced, leaving severe spasm. This mimics a frank (persistent) dislocation requiring reduction, an acute ligament rupture and (given effusion) a septic joint. Reassuring features are normal joint contour with no stuck-out deformity, intact distal neurovascular status (warm, normal colour, sensation and movement), and no fever/single hot joint. However, telehealth cannot examine or reduce joints or assess neurovascular status, and the patient is immobile and isolated (fall/self-care risk). The correct action is urgent in-person review (± imaging for occult tears) with analgesia, joint support/bracing, physiotherapy and fall-safety — not remote benzodiazepines/cannabinoids — with 000/ED for a joint that stays out/deformed or a cold, blue or numb limb.

differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent subluxations reducing spontaneously with spasm on known hEDS.
Screen whether a joint is still out/deformed.
Stable tests and no fever argue against these, but occult tears may need imaging.
No stuck-out deformity, intact neurovascular status, no fever/hot joint — a reduced-subluxation flare.
Low probability but retained as a safety-net (unexaminable remotely).
Consider imaging for occult labral/meniscal injury.
No fever/single hot joint; mechanically-linked pain.
Working telehealth diagnosis: reduced-subluxation flare needing urgent in-person review (± imaging), analgesia/bracing/physiotherapy, and fall-safety, with dislocation/neurovascular safety-netting.

red_flags.description: A joint that stays out of place/deformed and won't reduce (frank dislocation)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A persistent dislocation needs prompt reduction to avoid neurovascular injury/avascular necrosis.

red_flags.description: A limb becoming cold, blue, pale or numb (neurovascular compromise)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Neurovascular compromise is a limb-threatening emergency.

red_flags.description: Fever or a single hot/red/very swollen joint (septic arthritis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A septic joint requires urgent aspiration.

red_flags.description: Immobile on the floor, unable to transfer/reach fluids while isolated (fall/self-care risk)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Immobility while alone risks a long lie, dehydration and further injury.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening whether a joint is still out or the limb is cold/numb.
Prescribing diazepam or cannabinoids over telehealth.
Not addressing that she is stuck on the floor and isolated.
Not arranging imaging where labral/meniscal tears are suspected.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Joints reduced, no deformity, intact neurovascular status — a reduced-subluxation flare
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports supportive management with in-person review and dislocation/neurovascular safety-netting.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen frank dislocation/neurovascular compromise and septic joint; advise analgesia, joint support/bracing, rest and fall-safety; arrange urgent in-person review (± imaging); escalate to ED/000 for a joint stuck out, a cold/blue/numb limb, or a septic joint.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Managing remotely without screening could miss a persistent dislocation or neurovascular compromise, or leave an immobile isolated patient at risk.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recurrent subluxations are managed with bracing, physiotherapy and analgesia; dislocations with neurovascular compromise are emergencies.
A persistently dislocated joint or neurovascular compromise requires urgent reduction/assessment; a hot joint requires aspiration to exclude sepsis.
